Practica Arboles Decision

Lucas Poveda

2023-12-11

Utiliza los datos de cáncer de mama que hemos trabajado en clase “breast_train_prep” y “breast_test_prep” (que puedes encontrar en el fichero “breast.Rdata” del Moodle) para crear un modelo predictivo usando CART y Bagged Trees. Compara los resultados con los obtenidos mediant KNN y LDA reportados en el bookdown del curso.

library(tidyverse)
## Warning: package 'tidyverse' was built under R version 4.3.1
## Warning: package 'forcats' was built under R version 4.3.1
## Warning: package 'lubridate' was built under R version 4.3.1

## ✔ lubridate 1.9.2     ✔ tidyr     1.3.0
## ✔ purrr     1.0.1     
## ── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──

## ✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()

library(caret)
## Warning: package 'caret' was built under R version 4.3.1
## Loading required package: lattice
## 
## Attaching package: 'caret'
## 
## The following object is masked from 'package:purrr':
## 
##     lift
library(rpart)  
library(rpart.plot)
## Warning: package 'rpart.plot' was built under R version 4.3.2

ARREGLO PARA QUE EL CODIGO DEL BOOKDOWN FUNCIONE

#setwd("C:/Users/lucas/Desktop/Universidad/4o/AA1")
load("breast.Rdata")
breast <- load("breast.Rdata")
new_names <- c("concave points_se","concave points_worst")
names(breast_train_prep)[names(breast_train_prep) %in% new_names] <- c("concave_points_se","concave_points_worst")
names(breast_test_prep)[names(breast_test_prep) %in% new_names] <- c("concave_points_se","concave_points_worst")

CART

skimr::skim(breast_train_prep)
Data summary
Name breast_train_prep
Number of rows 399
Number of columns 20
_______________________
Column type frequency:
factor 1
numeric 19
________________________
Group variables None

Variable type: factor

skim_variable n_missing complete_rate ordered n_unique top_counts
diagnosis 0 1 FALSE 2 B: 250, M: 149

Variable type: numeric

skim_variable n_missing complete_rate mean sd p0 p25 p50 p75 p100 hist
texture_mean 0 1 0 1 -2.02 -0.72 -0.12 0.63 4.80 ▅▇▃▁▁
area_mean 0 1 0 1 -1.46 -0.68 -0.29 0.39 5.28 ▇▃▂▁▁
smoothness_mean 0 1 0 1 -2.23 -0.70 -0.01 0.58 4.54 ▃▇▃▁▁
compactness_mean 0 1 0 1 -1.47 -0.77 -0.23 0.49 4.55 ▇▆▂▁▁
symmetry_mean 0 1 0 1 -2.68 -0.69 -0.07 0.53 4.38 ▁▇▆▁▁
fractal_dimension_mean 0 1 0 1 -1.81 -0.69 -0.16 0.50 4.99 ▆▇▂▁▁
texture_se 0 1 0 1 -1.65 -0.71 -0.23 0.51 5.32 ▇▆▂▁▁
area_se 0 1 0 1 -0.78 -0.51 -0.36 0.17 11.70 ▇▁▁▁▁
smoothness_se 0 1 0 1 -1.42 -0.60 -0.21 0.35 7.74 ▇▂▁▁▁
compactness_se 0 1 0 1 -1.28 -0.70 -0.27 0.40 6.33 ▇▃▁▁▁
concavity_se 0 1 0 1 -1.29 -0.68 -0.21 0.40 5.10 ▇▅▁▁▁
concave_points_se 0 1 0 1 -2.10 -0.69 -0.12 0.55 5.02 ▃▇▂▁▁
symmetry_se 0 1 0 1 -1.51 -0.65 -0.21 0.32 6.99 ▇▃▁▁▁
fractal_dimension_se 0 1 0 1 -1.33 -0.68 -0.19 0.40 6.63 ▇▃▁▁▁
smoothness_worst 0 1 0 1 -2.07 -0.70 -0.06 0.61 3.79 ▃▇▆▁▁
compactness_worst 0 1 0 1 -1.31 -0.69 -0.27 0.52 4.97 ▇▅▁▁▁
concave_points_worst 0 1 0 1 -1.73 -0.77 -0.25 0.74 2.69 ▅▇▅▃▁
symmetry_worst 0 1 0 1 -2.06 -0.62 -0.14 0.45 5.76 ▅▇▁▁▁
fractal_dimension_worst 0 1 0 1 -1.59 -0.67 -0.23 0.42 6.70 ▇▃▁▁▁

Creamos el modelo con el arbol completo, que luego podaremos:

model_full<-rpart(formula = diagnosis~., data=breast_train_prep, method="class"); model_full
## n= 399 
## 
## node), split, n, loss, yval, (yprob)
##       * denotes terminal node
## 
##  1) root 399 149 B (0.626566416 0.373433584)  
##    2) concave_points_worst< 0.5018401 273  27 B (0.901098901 0.098901099)  
##      4) area_mean< 0.1163047 249   9 B (0.963855422 0.036144578)  
##        8) concave_points_worst< -0.05205819 217   1 B (0.995391705 0.004608295) *
##        9) concave_points_worst>=-0.05205819 32   8 B (0.750000000 0.250000000)  
##         18) texture_mean< 0.4492966 21   1 B (0.952380952 0.047619048) *
##         19) texture_mean>=0.4492966 11   4 M (0.363636364 0.636363636) *
##      5) area_mean>=0.1163047 24   6 M (0.250000000 0.750000000) *
##    3) concave_points_worst>=0.5018401 126   4 M (0.031746032 0.968253968) *
rpart.plot(model_full, yesno=TRUE)

Usamos la siguiente tabla para decidir cómo podar el arbol:

printcp(model_full)
## 
## Classification tree:
## rpart(formula = diagnosis ~ ., data = breast_train_prep, method = "class")
## 
## Variables actually used in tree construction:
## [1] area_mean            concave_points_worst texture_mean        
## 
## Root node error: 149/399 = 0.37343
## 
## n= 399 
## 
##         CP nsplit rel error  xerror     xstd
## 1 0.791946      0   1.00000 1.00000 0.064847
## 2 0.080537      1   0.20805 0.24832 0.038885
## 3 0.010067      2   0.12752 0.18121 0.033673
## 4 0.010000      4   0.10738 0.22819 0.037429

Podaremos el arbol para el modelo que tiene un error de CV relativo más pequeño (menor xerror), es decir, nos quedamos con cp=0.010067

model_full_cart<-rpart(
  formula=diagnosis~.,
  data=breast_train_prep,
  method="class",
  cp=0.010067
)
print(model_full_cart)
## n= 399 
## 
## node), split, n, loss, yval, (yprob)
##       * denotes terminal node
## 
##  1) root 399 149 B (0.626566416 0.373433584)  
##    2) concave_points_worst< 0.5018401 273  27 B (0.901098901 0.098901099)  
##      4) area_mean< 0.1163047 249   9 B (0.963855422 0.036144578)  
##        8) concave_points_worst< -0.05205819 217   1 B (0.995391705 0.004608295) *
##        9) concave_points_worst>=-0.05205819 32   8 B (0.750000000 0.250000000)  
##         18) texture_mean< 0.4492966 21   1 B (0.952380952 0.047619048) *
##         19) texture_mean>=0.4492966 11   4 M (0.363636364 0.636363636) *
##      5) area_mean>=0.1163047 24   6 M (0.250000000 0.750000000) *
##    3) concave_points_worst>=0.5018401 126   4 M (0.031746032 0.968253968) *
plotcp(model_full_cart, upper="splits")

Podaremos el arbol con 4 divisiones

mdl_cart<-prune(model_full_cart, cp=model_full_cart$cptable[model_full_cart$cptable[,2]==3, "CP"])
rpart.plot(mdl_cart,yesno=TRUE)

Miramos la importancia de cada variable para este modelo

mdl_cart$variable.importance%>%
  data.frame()%>%
  rownames_to_column(var="Feature")%>%
  rename(Overall='.')%>%
  ggplot(aes(x=fct_reorder(Feature, Overall), y=Overall))+geom_pointrange(aes(ymin=0, ymax=Overall), color="cadetblue", size=.3)+theme_minimal()+coord_flip()+labs(x="",y="",title="Importancia mediante clasificacion simple")

summary(mdl_cart)
## Call:
## rpart(formula = diagnosis ~ ., data = breast_train_prep, method = "class", 
##     cp = 0.010067)

## 3 0.01006711      2 0.1275168 0.2080537 0.03588658
## 4 0.01006700      4 0.1073826 0.2416107 0.03840890

Evaluamos el modelo en la muestra test

pred<-predict(mdl_cart, newdata = breast_test_prep, type="class")
cm_mdlcart<-confusionMatrix(pred, breast_test_prep$diagnosis)
cm_mdlcart
## Confusion Matrix and Statistics
## 
##           Reference
## Prediction  B  M
##          B 95  3
##          M 12 60
##                                           
##                Accuracy : 0.9118          
##                  95% CI : (0.8586, 0.9498)
##     No Information Rate : 0.6294          
##     P-Value [Acc > NIR] : < 2e-16         
##                                           
##                   Kappa : 0.8163          
##                                           
##  Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 0.03887         
##                                           
##             Sensitivity : 0.8879          
##             Specificity : 0.9524          
##          Pos Pred Value : 0.9694          
##          Neg Pred Value : 0.8333          
##              Prevalence : 0.6294          
##          Detection Rate : 0.5588          
##    Detection Prevalence : 0.5765          
##       Balanced Accuracy : 0.9201          
##                                           
##        'Positive' Class : B               
## 
plot(breast_test_prep$diagnosis, pred, main="Clasificaciçon: Predicho vs Observado", xlab="Observado", ylab="Predicho")

Arboles de Regressión

Primero construimos un arbol completo y luego usamos validaciçon cruzada para podarlo

model_cart_full<-rpart(diagnosis~., breast_train_prep, method="anova");model_cart_full
## n= 399 
## 
## node), split, n, deviance, yval
##       * denotes terminal node
## 
##  1) root 399 93.3584000 1.373434  
##    2) concave_points_worst< 0.5018401 273 24.3296700 1.098901  
##      4) area_mean< 0.1163047 249  8.6746990 1.036145  
##        8) concave_points_worst< -0.05205819 217  0.9953917 1.004608 *
##        9) concave_points_worst>=-0.05205819 32  6.0000000 1.250000  
##         18) texture_mean< 0.4492966 21  0.9523810 1.047619 *
##         19) texture_mean>=0.4492966 11  2.5454550 1.636364 *
##      5) area_mean>=0.1163047 24  4.5000000 1.750000  
##       10) texture_mean< 0.0924265 11  2.7272730 1.454545 *
##       11) texture_mean>=0.0924265 13  0.0000000 2.000000 *
##    3) concave_points_worst>=0.5018401 126  3.8730160 1.968254  
##      6) area_se< -0.415271 7  1.7142860 1.571429 *
##      7) area_se>=-0.415271 119  0.9915966 1.991597 *
rpart.plot(model_cart_full, yesno=TRUE)

Buscamos el cp óptimo:

printcp(model_cart_full)
## 
## Regression tree:
## rpart(formula = diagnosis ~ ., data = breast_train_prep, method = "anova")
## 
## Variables actually used in tree construction:
## [1] area_mean            area_se              concave_points_worst
## [4] texture_mean        
## 
## Root node error: 93.358/399 = 0.23398
## 
## n= 399 
## 
##         CP nsplit rel error  xerror     xstd
## 1 0.697909      0   1.00000 1.00457 0.026351
## 2 0.119485      1   0.30209 0.35938 0.052167
## 3 0.022395      2   0.18261 0.24685 0.044622
## 4 0.018988      4   0.13782 0.24430 0.043995
## 5 0.012502      5   0.11883 0.23547 0.043278
## 6 0.010000      6   0.10633 0.21625 0.040545

Para encontrar el arbol con menor error error de predicción elejimos el que tiene SSE relativo más bajo (xerror), es decir, nos quedamos con cp=0.01

mdl_cart2<-prune(model_cart_full, cp=model_cart_full$cptable[model_cart_full$cptable[,2]==6,"CP"])
rpart.plot(mdl_cart2,yesno=TRUE)

mdl_cart2$variable.importance%>%
  data.frame()%>%
  rownames_to_column(var="diagnosis")%>%
  rename(Overall='.')%>%
  ggplot(aes(x=fct_reorder(diagnosis, Overall),y=Overall))+
  geom_pointrange(aes(ymin=0,ymax=Overall),color="cadetblue",size=.3)+
  theme_minimal()+
  coord_flip()+
  labs(x="",y="",title="Importancia de las variables con regression simple")

Predicción:

pred2<-predict(mdl_cart2, newdata = breast_test_prep, type="vector")
#cm_mdlcart2<-confusionMatrix(pred2, breast_test_prep$diagnosis)
#cm_mdlcart2

No he podido realizar esta predicción porque no encuentro dondte tengo el error. Se que me he equivocado porque el modelo deberia predecir el factor diagnosis, pero las predicciónes son numericos, asique en algun lugar debe haber un error

Bagged Tree

Este es un metodo que combina varios arboles de decision para mejorar el rendimiento predictivo.

Bagcontrol<-trainControl(
  method="cv",
  number=10,
  classProbs = TRUE,
  savePredictions="final"
)
mdl_bag<-train(
  diagnosis~.,
  data=breast_train_prep,
  method="treebag",
  trControl=Bagcontrol,
  metric="ROC"
)
## Warning in train.default(x, y, weights = w, ...): The metric "ROC" was not in
## the result set. Accuracy will be used instead.
mdl_bag$finalModel
## 
## Bagging classification trees with 25 bootstrap replications

Miramos como rinde en los datos test:

pred_bag<-predict(mdl_bag,newdata=breast_test_prep,type="raw")
cm_bag<-confusionMatrix(pred_bag,breast_test_prep$diagnosis)
cm_bag
## Confusion Matrix and Statistics
## 
##           Reference
## Prediction   B   M
##          B 102   3
##          M   5  60
##                                           
##                Accuracy : 0.9529          
##                  95% CI : (0.9094, 0.9795)
##     No Information Rate : 0.6294          
##     P-Value [Acc > NIR] : <2e-16          
##                                           
##                   Kappa : 0.8998          
##                                           
##  Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 0.7237          
##                                           
##             Sensitivity : 0.9533          
##             Specificity : 0.9524          
##          Pos Pred Value : 0.9714          
##          Neg Pred Value : 0.9231          
##              Prevalence : 0.6294          
##          Detection Rate : 0.6000          
##    Detection Prevalence : 0.6176          
##       Balanced Accuracy : 0.9528          
##                                           
##        'Positive' Class : B               
##

La curva ROC

#pred_bag2<-predict(mdl_bag,newdata=breast_test_prep,type="prob")
#roc.bag<-roc(breast_test_prep$diagnosis,pred_bag2,print.auc=TRUE,
          #   ci=TRUE,
          #   plot=TRUE)

El codigo da error porque no encuentra la metrica ROC en la funcion trainControl

Importancia de las variables:

#plot(VarImp(mdl_bag),main="Importancia de las variables con Bagging")

La funcion VarImp no la encuentra

Comparacion entre metodos:

#scoreboard<-data.frame(Modelo="Bagging",
                                        # Accurancy=cm_bag$overall["Accurancy"],
                                        # ROC=roc.bag$auc)%>%
 # arrange(desc(ROC))
#knit::kable(scoreboard,row.names=FALSE)

Como hemos tenido problemas con la metrica ROC, no podemos ejecutar esta parte del codigo

Bagging Arboles de Regresión

mdl_bag3<-train(
  diagnosis~.,
  data=breast_train_prep,
  method="treebag",
  trControl=Bagcontrol
)
mdl_bag3
## Bagged CART 
## 
## 399 samples
##  19 predictor
##   2 classes: 'B', 'M' 
## 
## No pre-processing
## Resampling: Cross-Validated (10 fold) 
## Summary of sample sizes: 359, 359, 359, 360, 359, 359, ... 
## Resampling results:
## 
##   Accuracy   Kappa    
##   0.9448718  0.8813088

Miramos el rendimiento en los datos test:

pred_bag3<-bind_cols(
  Predicted=predict(mdl_bag3,newdata=breast_test_prep),
  Actual=breast_test_prep$diagnosis
)

rmse_bag<-RMSE(pred=pred_bag3$Predicted, obs=pred_bag3$Actual)
## Warning in Ops.factor(pred, obs): '-' not meaningful for factors
pred_bag3%>%
  ggplot(aes(x=Actual, y=Predicted))+
  geom_point(alpha=0.6, color="cadetblue")+
  geom_smooth(method="loess", formula="y-x")+
  geom_abline(intercept=0, slope=1, linetype=2)+
  labs(title="Carseats Bagging, Predicted vs Actual (caret)")

La importancia de las variables son:

plot(varImp(mdl_bag3),main="Importancia de variables con Bagging")

Comparando:

scoreboard2<-data.frame(Modelo="Bagging",RMSE=rmse_bag)%>%arrange(RMSE)
                   knitr::kable(scoreboard2,row.names=FALSE)
Modelo RMSE
Bagging NA

Ahora quedaria comparar estos modelos con los de LDA y KNN, pero no puedo hacerlo ya que el codigo no se ejecuta
